thread.title: USG’s Programming Board votes to officially incorporate Undocumented Trojan Success Assembly – Annenberg Media
thread.title_full: USG’s Programming Board votes to officially incorporate Undocumented Trojan Success Assembly – Annenberg Media

title: USG’s Programming Board votes to officially incorporate Undocumented Trojan Success Assembly – Annenberg Media
text:
The Undergraduate Student Government’s Programing Board voted Tuesday to make the Undocumented Trojan Success Assembly (UTSA) an official assembly. The USG Senate will vote at its December 3 meeting on whether to finalize the assembly’s incorporation.
UTSA is a student government organization for undocumented students, students protected under the Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals policy, asylum seekers or any other noncitizen of the United States. The organization looks to provide community and assistance to these students.
UTSA has been under a trial period since the beginning of the fall after its predecessor, [IDEAS at USC](https://careers.usc.edu/organizations/ideas-at-usc-improving-dreams-equality-access-and-success/) (Improving Dreams, Equality, Access, and Success), an organization that sought to build networks and grows awareness of issues affecting students lacking permanent legal status, announced its intention to receive direct representation through USG.
Alexa Hernandez Diaz, undocumented senior journalism major and co-executive director of UTSA, said that the organization is pushing to establish a resource center and advocates for more opportunities for undocumented students.
“The heart of this is just the vision and the importance of having a platform—a space where undocumented students feel represented, where they feel safe and where they feel supported,” Hernandez Diaz said.
Becoming an official assembly would allow UTSA more access to support, university funding through USG and access to university administration, an anonymous UTSA member told Annenberg Media in [October](https://www.uscannenbergmedia.com/2024/10/15/undocumented-trojan-success-assembly-collects-messages-for-undocumented-students-at-its-first-major-event/).
“We’re committed to fighting for these centralized resources,” Herandez Diaz said.
As part of the Programming Board meeting, UTSA presented its case for official assembly status.
“I felt so alone and isolated,” said Diana Cervantes, co-executive director of UTSA and an undocumented student from Mexico. “If it wasn’t for UTSA, I wouldn’t know how to navigate this college experience.”
UTSA helps undocumented students navigate college admissions, academics and financial aid; as many do not qualify for scholarships. The assembly also raises awareness of students’ career and legal limitations, as employing undocumented individuals is illegal in the United States.
“They have been struggling, they have been pushing their heart and soul and that just shows how much it means to them,” said Elisa Garcia, a supervisor at the [First Generation Plus Success Center](https://firstgenplussc.usc.edu/). “Now more than ever, USC as an institution needs to recognize that its mission is to have them not only achieve a higher education but also pursue those careers that we see in amazing high stakes [environments].”
UTSA held several events this semester to raise awareness for the issues facing undocumented students, including bi-weekly “Undocu+ Wednesdays” events, Undocumented Student Action Week in October and the “Preparing for Trump’s Administration” presentation held on Wednesday.
“We want people to acknowledge that a lot of these students during their college experience have this mentality of surviving, because of the fears of deportation or just the migrational policies that are there,” Hernandez Diaz said. “Our goal is to have these students thrive in these spaces and their education.”
